scenario: You get a text message: "URGENT: Your national ID has been suspended due to suspicious activity. Call 1-800-555-4823 immediately or face arrest."
explanation: Government agencies never suspend your ID by text message, and they never threaten immediate arrest over the phone. This is a classic impersonation scam. Delete the message immediately.
redFlags: Threatening arrest
Urgent pressure to act now
Unknown phone number
Government agencies do not text about this

scenario: Your bank sends you this email: "Dear customer, we noticed unusual activity. Please verify your account at www.mybank-secure-login.net within 24 hours to avoid suspension."
explanation: Real bank emails come from the bank's official domain (like @yourbank.com), not lookalike websites ending in .net with extra words. The fake web address is the biggest red flag here.
redFlags: Suspicious web address (not the real bank)
Urgency and time pressure
Threatening account suspension
Real banks never ask you to log in via email links

scenario: You receive a call: "Hello, I'm calling from Microsoft Support. We have detected a virus on your computer. Please allow me remote access to fix it for a small fee."
explanation: Microsoft and Apple never call you out of the blue about viruses on your computer. This is the 'tech support scam' — one of the most common scams targeting seniors. Hang up immediately.
redFlags: Unsolicited call from a tech company
Asking for remote access to your computer
Asking for payment over the phone
Companies do not monitor individual computers

scenario: Your friend sends a WhatsApp message: "Hi! I'm locked out of my account and need you to forward me the 6-digit code you just received on your phone. It's urgent!"
explanation: This is account takeover fraud. Scammers hack your friend's account, then use it to trick you. The 6-digit code is YOUR login code for WhatsApp or another service. Never share it.
redFlags: Request to forward a verification code
Urgency
Even trusted contacts can be hacked
No legitimate service asks for codes this way

scenario: You get a text from your electricity provider saying: "Your bill payment was successful. Your next bill is due on the 15th. View your account at the official company website."
explanation: This looks like a normal billing notification. It doesn't ask for immediate payment, doesn't create urgency, and directs you to their official website. Always verify the sender's number matches your provider.

scenario: You receive a phone call: "Congratulations! You have been selected to receive a free medical alert device. We just need your insurance number to ship it to you at no cost."
explanation: This is medical fraud. Scammers use your health insurance number to bill for fake services. There are no truly free medical devices given over the phone. Hang up and call your provider directly if concerned.
redFlags: Asking for medical or insurance numbers
Something for free to create goodwill
Unsolicited call
Too good to be true offer

scenario: Your grandson calls you: "Grandma, I'm in trouble — I had an accident and I'm in jail. I need you to buy $2,000 in Amazon gift cards right now. Please don't tell mom and dad."
explanation: This is the 'grandparent scam'. Scammers impersonate grandchildren to create emotional panic. No real emergency ever requires payment in gift cards. Hang up and call your grandson directly on his known phone number.
redFlags: Asking for gift cards (never a real payment method for emergencies)
Secrecy — asking you not to tell family
Emotional manipulation and urgency
Police and jails never accept gift card payments

scenario: You get a text: "Delivery Courier: Your package could not be delivered. Update your delivery address by clicking here: delivery-update-secure.com"
explanation: Real delivery texts come from official systems and link to the official company website. The web address provided is a fake site designed to steal your password and credit card details.
redFlags: Web address is not the official courier domain
Creating urgency about a package
Couriers never ask you to re-enter delivery info via suspicious SMS links

scenario: Your doctor's office calls to confirm: "This is a reminder call from Dr. Smith's clinic. Your appointment is tomorrow, Tuesday at 10:30 AM. Press 1 to confirm or 2 to cancel."
explanation: This is a standard appointment reminder call. It doesn't ask for personal information, payment, or account numbers. It only asks you to confirm or cancel an existing appointment you booked.

scenario: A WhatsApp message from an unknown number: "Hi aunty, this is my new number. I lost my old phone. Can you send me some money via a payment app? I will return it tomorrow. Very urgent."
explanation: This is digital payment fraud using social engineering. Scammers claim to be a relative with a new number to create trust. Always verify by calling the person on their old number or through a family member. Never send money to unverified numbers.
redFlags: Unknown number claiming to be family
Urgency and time pressure
Immediate money request
Asks for direct digital transfer — hard to reverse

scenario: You get a WhatsApp message: "Dear customer, your bank KYC (Know Your Customer) is expired. Your account will be blocked in 24 hours. Update now by clicking: bank-kyc-update.net/verify"
explanation: Banks never ask you to update official documents by clicking a WhatsApp link. The website provided is fake. Always go directly to your bank's official app or visit the branch.
redFlags: Bank details never updated via WhatsApp link
Fake website address
Urgency and 24-hour deadline to create panic

scenario: A stranger sends you a tiny amount on a payment app with the message: 'Wrong transfer, please return the money as I sent you a large amount by mistake.'
explanation: This is a very common digital payment scam. The scammer sends a tiny amount to build trust, then claims they sent a large amount by mistake. There was no larger transfer — you will lose money if you pay. Always check your actual bank balance before refunding anyone.
redFlags: Claims to have sent more money than you actually received
Pressures you to refund quickly
Sending a small amount first to build false trust

scenario: You receive an SMS: "Your ID has been linked to illegal activities. Call the Cyber Police officer on this number within 2 hours to avoid arrest. Case no: CP/2024/XXXX"
explanation: The police never send SMS messages threatening arrest over the phone. This is the 'Digital Arrest' scam — where criminals impersonate government officers. Never call the number in such messages.
redFlags: Government agencies never threaten arrest via SMS
Urgent 2-hour deadline to create panic
Fake case numbers to appear official
